# Verify that sesam tell adds a user (key re-wrapped) and kill removes them
# (key rotated), and that the audit log remains intact throughout.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Sanity check: admin can already show the audit log.
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"operation"'
stdout '"init"'

# Tell: add bob as a dev. Passes bob's public key as recipient.
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --group dev

# The audit log now has the user.tell entry.
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.tell"'
stdout '"bob"'

# Bob's identity can now decrypt the audit log key (WriteAuditKey ran).
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.tell"'
stdout '"bob"'

# Kill: remove bob. This rotates the key so bob loses access.
exec sesam kill --user bob

# Admin can still load the log and sees the kill entry.
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.kill"'
stdout '"bob"'

# Bob can no longer decrypt the log (key was rotated out).
! exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
